docs.title: MTG AI
docs.text: Self-hosted competitive Commander (cEDH) deck-critique assistant: RAG over Scryfall/MTGJSON/meta data with a deterministic rules engine and a swappable generation backend.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install the package
pip install mtg-ai

# Or install with development dependencies
uv sync --all-extras

docs.title: CLI Usage
docs.text:
# Show help
mtg_ai --help

# Example command
mtg_ai hello --name "World"

docs.title: OpenSSF Compliance Guide
docs.text: This project follows OpenSSF (Open Source Security Foundation) security best practices, achieving high scores on the Scorecard and Best Practices Badge.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Estimated Scorecard Score: 9.0/10 (Excellent) Best Practices Compliance: 95%+ (Passing)

This template implements comprehensive security controls including:

  • ✅ Signed releases with Sigstore/Cosign
  • ✅ SLSA Level 3 provenance attestations
  • ✅ Continuous fuzzing with ClusterFuzzLite
  • ✅ Branch protection with scoped permissions
  • ✅ Automated dependency updates
  • ✅ Comprehensive security scanning

docs.title: 1. Signed Releases 🔐
docs.text:
All releases are cryptographically signed using Sigstore/Cosign for keyless signing.

What it provides:

  • Cryptographic proof of authenticity
  • Verification that releases come from this repository
  • Protection against supply chain attacks
  • Certificate transparency

Workflow: .github/workflows/release.yml

How to verify a release:

# Install cosign
brew install cosign  # macOS
# or download from https://github.com/sigstore/cosign/releases

# Download release artifacts
wget https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai/releases/download/v1.0.0/package.whl
wget https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai/releases/download/v1.0.0/package.whl.sig
wget https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai/releases/download/v1.0.0/package.whl.pem

# Verify signature
cosign verify-blob \
  --certificate package.whl.pem \
  --signature package.whl.sig \
  --certificate-identity-regexp="https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai" \
  --certificate-oidc-issuer="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
  package.whl

docs.title: 2. SLSA Provenance 🔗
docs.text:
SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) Level 3 provenance attestations are generated for all releases.

What it provides:

  • Build environment details
  • Source repository verification
  • Complete build reproducibility metadata
  • Independent verification of the build process

Workflow: .github/workflows/release.yml (provenance job)

How to verify provenance:

# Download provenance file
wget https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai/releases/download/v1.0.0/attestation.intoto.jsonl

# Verify with slsa-verifier
slsa-verifier verify-artifact package.whl \
  --provenance-path attestation.intoto.jsonl \
  --source-uri github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai

docs.title: 3. Continuous Fuzzing 🐛
docs.text:
Automated fuzzing with ClusterFuzzLite discovers security vulnerabilities.

What it provides:

  • Discovery of crashes and hangs
  • Memory safety violations (via AddressSanitizer)
  • Edge case detection
  • Security vulnerability identification

Workflow: .github/workflows/cifuzzy.yml Fuzz Harnesses: fuzz/ directory

How to run locally:

# Install Atheris
uv pip install atheris

# Run input validation fuzzer (60 seconds)
python fuzz/fuzz_input_validation.py -max_total_time=60

View fuzzing results:

  • GitHub Security tab → Code scanning alerts
  • Workflow artifacts (crash samples)

docs.title: 4. Branch Protection 🛡️
docs.text:
Enforced branch protection rules prevent unauthorized changes.

Protection rules:

  • ✅ Required status checks (CI / CI Pipeline, Security Analysis / Security Scan, PR Validation)
  • ✅ Required pull request reviews (1 approval)
  • ✅ Code owner reviews required
  • ✅ Dismiss stale reviews
  • ✅ Enforce for administrators
  • ✅ Linear history required
  • ✅ Force pushes blocked
  • ✅ Branch deletions blocked
  • ✅ Conversation resolution required

Setup:

# Set GitHub token
export GITHUB_TOKEN=<YOUR_GITHUB_TOKEN>

# Run setup script
python scripts/setup_github_protection.py

Manual setup:

  1. Go to: https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/mtg_ai/settings/branches
  2. Click "Add branch protection rule"
  3. Apply protection pattern: main
  4. Enable all recommended protections

docs.title: 5. Scoped Workflow Permissions 🔑
docs.text:
All GitHub Actions workflows use least-privilege permissions.

Principle: Grant only the minimum permissions needed for each job.

Implementation:

permissions:
  contents: read        # For checkout
  pull-requests: write  # For PR comments
  checks: write         # For check runs

Not this:

permissions: read-all  # ❌ Too permissive

docs.title: 6. Dependency Management 📦
docs.text:
Automated dependency updates and vulnerability scanning.

Tools:

  • Renovate: Automated dependency updates
  • Safety: Python dependency CVE scanning
  • OSV-Scanner: Multi-ecosystem vulnerability detection
  • Google Assured OSS: Vetted, secure packages

Configuration:

  • renovate.json - Update automation
  • osv-scanner.toml - Vulnerability exceptions
  • .env.example - Assured OSS configuration

docs.title: 7. Security Scanning (SAST) 🔍
docs.text:
Comprehensive static analysis on every commit.

Tools:

  • Ruff: Python linting with security rules
  • Bandit: Python security issue detection
  • BasedPyright: Type safety (prevents entire classes of bugs)
  • CodeQL: Advanced semantic code analysis

Workflow: .github/workflows/security-analysis.yml

docs.title: 8. Mutation Testing 🧬
docs.text:
Ensures test quality with mutmut.

What it does:

  • Mutates source code (changes operators, values, etc.)
  • Runs tests against mutated code
  • Verifies tests catch the mutations
  • Measures test effectiveness

Configuration: pyproject.toml → [tool.mutmut]

Usage:

# Run mutation testing
mutmut run

# View results
mutmut results

# Show specific mutations
mutmut show

docs.title: Project Planning with Claude Code
docs.text: This template includes an integrated AI-assisted project planning workflow that transforms your project concept into actionable development plans with proper git branch strategy.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The planning workflow generates 4 core documents, then synthesizes them into a comprehensive project plan:

Project Description
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Step 1: Generate Planning Docs      │
│   (project-planning skill)            │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│   project-vision.md (PVS)             │  What & Why
│   tech-spec.md                        │  How (architecture)
│   roadmap.md                          │  When (phases)
│   adr/adr-001-*.md                    │  Key decisions
└───────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Step 2: Synthesize Project Plan     │
│   (project-plan-synthesizer agent)    │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│   PROJECT-PLAN.md                     │  Actionable plan
│   - Git branch strategy               │  with semantic
│   - Phase deliverables                │  release alignment
│   - TodoWrite checklist               │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Step 3: Start Development           │
│   /git/milestone start                │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘

docs.title: Step 1: Generate Planning Documents
docs.text:
Describe your project and generate the 4 core documents:

# Using the /plan command (recommended)
/plan I want to build a CLI tool for managing personal finances.
The main users are individuals tracking expenses.
Core features: expense tracking, budget categories, monthly reports.
Technical constraints: must work offline, SQLite for storage.

Or provide a more detailed description:

Generate planning documents for this project:

I'm building a REST API for inventory management. Target users are
small business owners. Core features include product CRUD, stock
tracking, low-stock alerts, and reporting. Must integrate with
existing PostgreSQL database and support OAuth2 authentication.

What gets created:

Document Location Purpose Project Vision & Scope docs/planning/project-vision.md Problem, solution, scope, success metrics Technical Specification docs/planning/tech-spec.md Architecture, data model, APIs, security Development Roadmap docs/planning/roadmap.md Phased implementation plan Architecture Decision Records docs/planning/adr/adr-001-*.md Key technical decisions with rationale

The project-planning skill will: 1. Analyze your project description 2. Generate each document using templates 3. Validate each document with AI consensus review 4. Ensure documents cross-reference correctly 5. Flag any assumptions needing your validation

docs.title: Step 2: Synthesize into Project Plan
docs.text:
After the 4 documents are generated, synthesize them into an actionable plan:

# Request synthesis
"Synthesize my planning documents into a project plan"

The project-plan-synthesizer agent will: 1. Read and validate all 4 source documents 2. Extract key information from each document 3. Lookup best practices via Context7 for your tech stack 4. Map phases to semantic release-aligned git branches 5. Validate with tiered consensus (multi-model AI review) 6. Generate docs/planning/PROJECT-PLAN.md 7. Create initial TodoWrite checklist for Phase 0

Output: docs/planning/PROJECT-PLAN.md containing: - Git branch strategy for each phase - Consolidated risk register - Cross-referenced architecture decisions - Success criteria per phase - TodoWrite checklist for Phase 0

docs.title: Git Branch Strategy (Semantic Release)
docs.text:
Phases are automatically mapped to semantic release branch types:

Phase Focus Branch Type Version Impact Example Foundation/Setup feat/ Minor (0.X.0) feat/phase-0-foundation Core Features feat/ Minor (0.X.0) feat/phase-1-core Additional Features feat/ Minor (0.X.0) feat/phase-2-advanced Performance perf/ Patch (0.0.X) perf/phase-3-optimization Documentation docs/ None docs/phase-4-documentation Bug Fixes fix/ Patch (0.0.X) fix/phase-X-bugfixes

docs.title: Step 3: Review and Start Development
docs.text:
Review the synthesized plan:

# Review the generated plan
cat docs/planning/PROJECT-PLAN.md

# Verify:
# - Phase deliverables match your expectations
# - Git branch types align with semantic release
# - Success criteria are measurable

Start development with the milestone workflow:

# Start Phase 0 (creates branch, sets up tracking)
/git/milestone start feat/phase-0-foundation

This will: - Create the feature branch from main - Set up git worktree if parallel development is needed - Show semantic release impact for this branch type - Create TodoWrite list from phase deliverables

docs.title: Phase Completion Workflow
docs.text:
When you complete a phase:

# 1. Validate all commits match branch type
/git/milestone complete

# 2. Create PR with What the Diff summary
/git/pr-prepare --include_wtd=true

# 3. Merge PR (triggers semantic release if applicable)

# 4. Start next phase
/git/milestone start feat/phase-1-core

Validate code against specs:

Review this code against tech-spec.md section 6 (security).

Check phase progress:

Review PROJECT-PLAN.md Phase 1 deliverables and update status.

Qlty aggregates code quality metrics (complexity scores, code smell detection, and coverage trends) and surfaces them as PR comments via the qlty.yml workflow. It does not replace Ruff, BasedPyright, or pytest; those tools run directly in CI and Qlty consumes their output to build trend data.

docs.title: Branch Protection Rules
docs.text: You can configure branch protection either via script (recommended) or manually through the GitHub UI.

docs.title: Option 1: Automated Setup (Recommended)
docs.text:
Use the included script to configure comprehensive branch protection:

# Set up branch protection with default settings
uv run python scripts/setup_github_protection.py

# Or specify custom settings
uv run python scripts/setup_github_protection.py --enforce-admins --require-code-owner-reviews

The script configures: - Required pull request reviews before merging - Required status checks to pass - Enforce rules for administrators - Require signed commits - Dismiss stale reviews on new commits

docs.title: Option 2: Manual UI Setup
docs.text:
1. Go to Repository Settings > Branches > Add rule
  2. Apply to: main
  3. Enable:
  4. Require a pull request before merging
  5. Require status checks to pass
  6. Require branches to be up to date
  7. Include administrators

docs.title: Automated Setup Script
docs.text:
The template includes scripts/setup_github_protection.py, which configures branch protection via the GitHub API with the project's required status checks, required signatures, code-owner reviews, and the chosen approval count (controlled by the sole_contributor cookiecutter flag). Selecting sole_contributor=yes also disables required code-owner reviews, because the GitHub API rejects the combination of zero required approvers and required code-owner review with HTTP 422.

Manual invocation after creating the GitHub repo:

export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_your_token_with_admin_repo_scope
uv run python scripts/setup_github_protection.py

Prerequisites:

  • GitHub repository must exist (create it via gh repo create or the web UI).
  • GITHUB_TOKEN must have the repo scope (or repository admin permission for fine-grained PATs).
  • The local origin remote must point at the new GitHub repo.

If you set the auto_setup_branch_protection cookiecutter variable to yes when generating the project, and you supply GITHUB_TOKEN in your environment, and the project has a configured remote, the post-gen hook runs this script automatically. The auto-run is non-fatal: if any precondition fails or the API call errors out, generation completes with a printed warning and you can run the script manually later.

docs.title: Prerequisites for auto-run
docs.text:
The post-gen hook runs git init against the freshly generated project, which creates a repo with no origin remote. The auto-run therefore only succeeds when all of the following are true at generation time:

  1. auto_setup_branch_protection is set to yes in your cookiecutter answers.
  2. GITHUB_TOKEN is exported in the shell that runs cruft create (or cookiecutter), with repo scope (or repo-admin permission for a fine-grained PAT).

  3. An origin remote is configured on the generated project before the auto-run reaches the helper. In the default flow this is not the case, so the auto-run prints a hint and skips. Two ways to satisfy the requirement:

  4. Pre-create the GitHub repo and the local remote. Create the empty GitHub repo first (gh repo create <name> --public), then add the remote manually after generation completes:

    cd <project_slug>
git remote add origin git@github.com:<owner>/<name>.git
GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxx uv run python scripts/setup_github_protection.py

  5. Skip auto-run and use the manual command later. Leave auto_setup_branch_protection=no (the default) and run the script manually once the remote is in place. This is the documented happy path for most users.

When the auto-run is skipped because a precondition is missing, the hook prints a one-line hint identifying the missing piece (token, remote, or script) along with the exact command to recover. No GITHUB_TOKEN value is ever echoed back.

docs.title: Python Version Compatibility Guide
docs.text: This project supports Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14 with full testing across all versions.

docs.title: Version Support Matrix
docs.text: Python Version Support Status Nox Testing CI Testing Notes 3.10 ✅ Supported ✅ All versions ✅ Full CI/CD Minimum version, requires backports 3.11 ✅ Supported ✅ All versions ✅ Full CI/CD LTS version (EOL Oct 2027) 3.12 ✅ Supported ✅ All versions ✅ Full CI/CD Default/recommended version 3.13 ✅ Supported ✅ All versions ✅ Full CI/CD Latest stable, PEP 594 removals 3.14 ✅ Supported ✅ All versions ✅ Full CI/CD Latest (Oct 2025), free-threaded, JIT 3.15+ ⚠️ Not tested ❌ None ❌ No CI/CD May work but not guaranteed

docs.title: Python 3.10 Support (Backports Needed)
docs.text: Since Python 3.10 is the minimum version, some features from newer versions require backport packages:

docs.title: Required Backports for 3.10
docs.text:
dependencies = [
    # TOML support (tomllib added in 3.11)
    "tomli>=2.0.0; python_version < '3.11'",

    # Exception groups (added in 3.11)
    "exceptiongroup>=1.1.0; python_version < '3.11'",

    # Newer typing features
    "typing-extensions>=4.12.0",  # Always useful for backporting latest typing features
]

docs.title: Code Patterns for 3.10 Compatibility
docs.text:
# TOML parsing
import sys
if sys.version_info >= (3, 11):
    import tomllib
else:
    import tomli as tomllib

# Or use try/except
try:
    import tomllib
except ModuleNotFoundError:
    import tomli as tomllib

# Exception groups (use exceptiongroup backport for 3.10)
try:
    ExceptionGroup
except NameError:
    from exceptiongroup import ExceptionGroup

# Typing features
from typing_extensions import Self, TypeVarTuple, Never

docs.title: Built-in Features (Python 3.11+)
docs.text: The following features are natively available in Python 3.11+ and do not require backport packages:

docs.title: Standard Library Additions
docs.text:
• tomllib: Native TOML parser (PEP 680)
import tomllib  # No need for tomli backport

with open("pyproject.toml", "rb") as f:
    data = tomllib.load(f)
  • asyncio.TaskGroup: Structured concurrency (PEP 654)
import asyncio

async def main():
    async with asyncio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        tg.create_task(task1())
        tg.create_task(task2())
  • ExceptionGroup: Native exception groups (PEP 654)
try:
    ...
except* ValueError as eg:  # Note the * syntax
    handle_value_errors(eg)

docs.title: Typing Improvements
docs.text:
• typing.Self: Self-referential type annotations (PEP 673)
from typing import Self

class Builder:
    def set_value(self, value: int) -> Self:
        self.value = value
        return self
  • typing.TypeVarTuple: Variadic generics (PEP 646)
  • typing.Never: Bottom type (PEP 484)
  • typing.LiteralString: Literal string type (PEP 675)

docs.title: Other Enhancements
docs.text:
• Fine-grained error locations in tracebacks
  • Faster startup time (10-60% improvement)
  • Better exception notes with .add_note()

docs.title: PEP 594: Module Removals
docs.text: Python 3.13 removed the following deprecated modules. If your code uses them, you'll need replacements:

docs.title: Removed Modules
docs.text: Module Purpose Replacement cgi, cgitb CGI scripts Use legacy-cgi package or modern web frameworks imghdr, sndhdr Image/sound header detection Use filetype or python-magic mailcap Mailcap file handling Use mailcap-fix package nntplib NNTP protocol Use nntplib-py3 package telnetlib Telnet protocol Use telnetlib3 package uu UU encoding Use base64 module aifc, audioop, chunk, sunau Audio file handling Use audiofile or specialized libraries crypt Unix password hashing Use bcrypt or passlib msilib Windows Installer Windows-specific, use pywin32 nis, spwd, ossaudiodev Unix-specific modules Platform-specific alternatives pipes Shell command pipelines Use subprocess module xdrlib XDR data Use xdrlib3 package

docs.title: How to Handle Removals
docs.text:
If you do use these modules, add conditional dependencies:

# In pyproject.toml dependencies list
dependencies = [
    # ... other deps ...

    # Add replacements for Python 3.13+
    "legacy-cgi>=2.6.1; python_version >= '3.13'",  # For cgi module
    "filetype>=1.2.0",  # Better alternative to imghdr/sndhdr
    "python-magic>=0.4.27",  # Alternative file type detection
]

docs.title: Python 3.13 Improvements
docs.text:
• Experimental free-threaded mode (no GIL) with python3.13t
  • Just-in-Time (JIT) compiler (experimental, opt-in)
  • Improved error messages with better suggestions
  • Better typing support for generics

docs.title: Python 3.14 Changes
docs.text: Python 3.14.0 was released on October 7, 2025, with significant performance and concurrency improvements.

docs.title: Free-Threaded Python (PEP 779)
docs.text:
Python 3.14 officially supports free-threaded mode (no Global Interpreter Lock):

# Install free-threaded Python
uv python install 3.14t

# Check if GIL is disabled
python -c "import sys; print(f'GIL enabled: {sys._is_gil_enabled()}')"

Important Considerations: - Not all packages support free-threaded mode yet - Some C extensions require GIL - Performance may vary - benchmark your workload - Use standard Python 3.14 unless you specifically need multi-threading

docs.title: Deferred Annotation Evaluation (PEP 649)
docs.text:
Breaking Change: Annotations are no longer evaluated at function definition time.

# Python 3.13 and earlier
def func(x: expensive_type_check()):  # Evaluated immediately
    pass

# Python 3.14
def func(x: expensive_type_check()):  # Deferred until introspection
    pass

Impact on Runtime Type Checking: - Libraries like Pydantic and dataclasses handle this automatically - Custom type introspection code may need updates - Access annotations via __annotations__ or inspect.get_annotations()

docs.title: Template Strings (PEP 750)
docs.text:
Python 3.14 adds template strings (t-strings):

name = "world"
message = t"Hello {name}"  # New syntax

This project does not require t-strings - standard f-strings work across all versions.

docs.title: Deprecations
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations is deprecated: - This template's check_type_hints.py currently enforces this import - Deprecation in 3.14, removal not before Python 3.13 EOL (2029) - Continue using it for now (required for 3.10 compatibility) - We'll update the template before 2029

NotImplemented Boolean Context: - Using NotImplemented in boolean contexts now raises TypeError - Was DeprecationWarning since Python 3.9

docs.title: New Features
docs.text:
• compression.zstd module: Native Zstandard compression
  • pathlib enhancements: Recursive copy/move methods
  • Experimental JIT compiler: Included in official binaries
  • Better error messages: More context and suggestions
  • Syntax highlighting: In default interactive shell
  • Android platform support: Official binary releases
  • Emscripten support: Tier 3 platform support

docs.title: Testing with Python 3.14

# Install Python 3.14
uv python install 3.14

# Run tests with 3.14
uv run --python 3.14 pytest

# Test all versions including 3.14

docs.title: Conditional Dependencies by Python Version
docs.text:
UV automatically installs the correct packages based on Python version:

dependencies = [
    # Install only on older Python versions (if you support < 3.11)
    "tomli>=2.0.0; python_version < '3.11'",
    "exceptiongroup>=1.1.0; python_version < '3.11'",

    # Install only on newer Python versions
    "legacy-cgi>=2.6.1; python_version >= '3.13'",

    # Install on specific version ranges
    "typing-extensions>=4.12.0; python_version < '3.13'",
]

docs.title: Platform-Specific Dependencies
docs.text:
Combine version and platform markers:

dependencies = [
    # Windows-specific for Python 3.13+
    "pywin32>=306; sys_platform == 'win32' and python_version >= '3.13'",

    # Unix-specific backport
    "unix-helpers>=1.0; sys_platform != 'win32'",
]

docs.title: Environment Markers Reference
docs.text:
Marker Example Description python_version python_version >= '3.11' Python version comparison python_full_version python_full_version == '3.11.2' Exact version match sys_platform sys_platform == 'linux' Operating system platform_machine platform_machine == 'x86_64' CPU architecture platform_system platform_system == 'Darwin' OS name (Darwin=macOS) implementation_name implementation_name == 'cpython' Python implementation

See: PEP 508 - Dependency specification for Python Software Packages

docs.title: Modern Union Syntax (3.10+)
docs.text:
This project requires the from __future__ import annotations import when using | union syntax:

from __future__ import annotations

def process(data: str | bytes) -> int | None:
    """This is enforced by scripts/check_type_hints.py"""
    ...

Why? While Python 3.10+ supports | natively, the future import:

  • Ensures forward compatibility
  • Makes runtime type evaluation consistent
  • Improves code clarity across versions
  • Required by our CI checks

Validation: Run python scripts/check_type_hints.py --fix to automatically add missing imports.

docs.title: Typing Best Practices
docs.text:
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Self

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    # Import types only for type checking (avoids circular imports)
    from mypackage.models import User

class UserManager:
    def create(self, name: str) -> Self:
        """Returns same type as the class."""
        return type(self)()

    def get_user(self, user_id: int) -> User:
        """Uses forward reference safely."""
        ...

docs.title: Migrating to Python 3.10+
docs.text:
If migrating from Python 3.9 or earlier:

  1. Update Python version:
# Install Python 3.10+
uv python install 3.10

# Update requires-python in pyproject.toml
requires-python = ">=3.10,<3.15"
  1. Add backport packages for 3.10 compatibility:
uv add "tomli>=2.0.0; python_version < '3.11'"
uv add "exceptiongroup>=1.1.0; python_version < '3.11'"
uv add "typing-extensions>=4.12.0"
  1. Use conditional imports for 3.10/3.11+ compatibility:
# TOML parsing - works on 3.10 and 3.11+
try:
    import tomllib
except ModuleNotFoundError:
    import tomli as tomllib
  1. Test across versions:
nox -s test

docs.title: Upgrading from Python 3.10 to 3.11+
docs.text:
When dropping 3.10 support in the future:

  1. Remove backport packages:
uv remove tomli exceptiongroup
  1. Update imports to use native modules:
# Before (3.10 compat)
try:
    import tomllib
except ModuleNotFoundError:
    import tomli as tomllib

# After (3.11+ only)
import tomllib
  1. Update requires-python:
requires-python = ">=3.11,<3.15"

docs.title: Preparing for Python 3.14
docs.text:
Python 3.14 (expected October 2025):

  • Monitor deprecation warnings: python -W default
  • Review PEPs targeting 3.14
  • Test with pre-release versions: uv python install 3.14.0a1

docs.title: Import Errors on Python 3.13

uv add "legacy-cgi>=2.6.1; python_version >= '3.13'"

Solution: Add future import:

from __future__ import annotations

Or run auto-fix:

python scripts/check_type_hints.py --fix

docs.title: mtg_ai.utils.logging
docs.text:
Structured logging configuration using structlog and rich.

Provides JSON-formatted logs for production environments with rich console output for development. All logs include contextual information for debugging and monitoring.

Features: - Rich console output for development - JSON-formatted logs for production - Correlation ID support for distributed tracing - Performance logging helpers

Note: Module named "logging" intentionally shadows stdlib for project-specific configuration.

docs.title: setup_logging(level='INFO', json_logs=False, include_timestamp=True, include_correlation=True)
docs.text:
Configure structured logging for the application.

Sets up both standard logging and structlog with appropriate handlers for the environment (JSON for production, rich console for development).

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default level str

Logging level (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL). Defaults to INFO.

'INFO' json_logs bool

If True, output JSON logs (production mode). If False, use rich console formatting (development mode). Defaults to False.

False include_timestamp bool

Whether to include timestamps in log output. Defaults to True.

True include_correlation bool

Whether to include correlation IDs from request context in log output. Defaults to True. Requires API framework.

True Example

docs.title: Development setup
docs.text: setup_logging(level="DEBUG", json_logs=False)

docs.title: Production setup with correlation
docs.text: setup_logging(level="INFO", json_logs=True, include_correlation=True)

docs.title: get_logger(name)
docs.text:
Get a structured logger instance.

Creates or retrieves a structlog logger with the given name. This should typically be called with name to create module-specific loggers.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default name str

Logger name (typically name of the module).

required

Returns:

Type Description BoundLogger

Configured structlog logger instance with methods like:

BoundLogger
  • logger.info()
BoundLogger
  • logger.debug()
BoundLogger
  • logger.warning()
BoundLogger
  • logger.error()
BoundLogger
  • logger.exception()
Example

logger = get_logger(name) logger.info("Processing started", document_id="doc_001") logger.warning("Low confidence", confidence=0.45, threshold=0.5) logger.error("Processing failed", error="file_not_found")

docs.title: log_performance(logger, operation, duration_ms, success=True, **context)
docs.text:
Log performance metrics for an operation.

Provides a standardized way to log performance data with operation name, duration, success status, and additional context.

Parameters:

Name Type Description Default logger BoundLogger

Structlog logger instance from get_logger().

required operation str

Name of the operation being timed.

required duration_ms float

Duration in milliseconds.

required success bool

Whether the operation succeeded. Defaults to True.

True **context object

Additional context key-value pairs to include in the log.

{} Example

logger = get_logger(name) log_performance( ... logger, ... operation="image_processing", ... duration_ms=42.5, ... success=True, ... image_count=10, ... output_dir="/results", ... )

This document tracks CVEs and security advisories that have been identified but cannot be immediately remediated. Entries must be reviewed within 60 days of the Discovered date. Any entry older than 60 days without reassessment blocks releases per the OpenSSF release gate policy.

docs.title: PYSEC-2022-42969 | py | Medium
docs.text: Field Value CVE ID PYSEC-2022-42969 Package py Affected Version 1.11.0 Fixed Version No fix available Severity Medium CVSS Score 7.5 Discovered 2026-05-21 Reassessment Due 2026-07-20 Blocking Release No

docs.title: Description
docs.text: ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service) vulnerability in the py package's path.LocalPath function via crafted input to the svnwc.status method.

docs.title: Impact on This Project
docs.text: The py package is a transitive dependency of interrogate (a docstring coverage tool) used only in the development environment. It is never present in production runtime dependencies. The vulnerable svnwc.status code path is exercised only when parsing Subversion repository info, which this project does not do.

docs.title: Remediation Plan
docs.text:
• Monitor upstream py package for a fix release (package is largely unmaintained)
  • Evaluate replacing interrogate with an alternative docstring coverage tool if no fix arrives by 2026-07-20 (reassessment due)

docs.title: Why Not Fixed Yet
docs.text: The py package has no released fix version. The package is largely unmaintained. interrogate has not released a version that drops the py dependency.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PYSEC-2022-42969
  • GitHub Advisory GHSA-w596-4wvx-j9j6

docs.title: PYSEC-2026-89 | markdown | High
docs.text: Field Value CVE ID PYSEC-2026-89 Package markdown Affected Version 3.10.2 Fixed Version Fixed only in 3.8.1-3.8.2; no fixed release available for >=3.9 as of 2026-05-21 Severity High CVSS Score 7.5 Discovered 2026-05-21 Reassessment Due 2026-07-20 Blocking Release No

docs.title: Description
docs.text: DoS via malformed HTML-like sequences that cause html.parser.HTMLParser to raise an unhandled AssertionError during Markdown parsing. Python-Markdown does not catch this exception, so any application parsing attacker-controlled Markdown may crash. Enables remote unauthenticated Denial of Service in web applications, documentation systems, CI/CD pipelines, and any service rendering untrusted Markdown. Also known as CVE-2025-69534 and GHSA-5wmx-573v-2qwq.

docs.title: Impact on This Project
docs.text: The markdown package is a transitive dependency of mkdocs, mkdocs-material, and related documentation tools. It is present only in the development environment (docs tooling). This project does not parse attacker-controlled Markdown input at runtime. The vulnerability is not exploitable in production. Dev-only impact.

docs.title: Remediation Plan
docs.text:
• Monitor the markdown package for a release that fixes PYSEC-2026-89 in the 3.9+ series
  • Evaluate pinning markdown to 3.8.2 if compatible with mkdocs and mkdocs-material dependencies
  • Reassess by 2026-07-20 whether a fixed version is available or whether mkdocs has migrated to a safe markdown version

docs.title: Why Not Fixed Yet
docs.text: The fix landed in markdown 3.8.1, but the package subsequently released 3.9 and 3.10.x without incorporating the fix (or re-introduced the vulnerable code path). As of 2026-05-21, no version in the 3.9+ series resolves this CVE. Downgrading to 3.8.2 may conflict with mkdocs-material and other tooling that requires 3.9+.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• PYSEC-2026-89
  • GitHub Advisory GHSA-5wmx-573v-2qwq

docs.title: Quick Reference
docs.text:
Tag assumptions that could cause production failures using three markers:

  • #CRITICAL - assumption that could cause outages or data loss
  • #ASSUME - assumption that could cause bugs
  • #EDGE - assumption about uncommon scenarios

Each marker must be paired with a #VERIFY instruction describing the defensive code or validation required.

docs.title: Mandatory Categories
docs.text:
Tagging is mandatory for:

  • Timing dependencies (state updates, async operations, race conditions)
  • External resources (API availability, file existence, network connectivity)
  • Data integrity (type safety at boundaries, null/undefined handling)
  • Concurrency (shared state, transaction isolation, deadlock potential)
  • Security (authentication, authorization, input validation)
  • Payment and financial (transaction integrity, retry logic, rollback handling)

docs.title: Template Feedback
docs.text:
Purpose: Document issues discovered in this project that should be addressed in the cookiecutter-python-template.

Generated From: cookiecutter-python-template v0.1.0 Project Created: PROJECT_CREATION_DATE

docs.title: How to Use This File
docs.text:
When working on this project, if you discover any issue that originates from the template itself (not project-specific), add it here with the following format:

### [Short Title]

- **Priority**: Critical / High / Medium / Low
- **Category**: [Configuration / Documentation / Tooling / Structure / CI/CD / Security / Other]
- **Discovered**: YYYY-MM-DD

**Issue**: [Clear description of what's wrong or missing]

**Context**: [How was this discovered? What were you trying to do?]

**Suggested Fix**: [What should the template do differently?]

**Affected Files**: [List template files that need changes]

docs.title: .markdownlint.json trips on multi-phase planning docs
docs.text:
• Priority: Low
  • Category: Configuration
  • Discovered: 2026-06-01

Issue: The shipped .markdownlint.json leaves MD024 (no-duplicate-heading) at its default and does not address MD060 (table-column-style). The planning documents produced by the /plan workflow (roadmap, ADRs) intentionally repeat sibling subheadings such as ### Objective and ### Deliverables under each phase, and use standard GFM tables. This generates dozens of editor warnings on freshly generated, valid docs.

Context: Discovered while generating docs/planning/ documents via /plan. Confirmed markdownlint is not part of the enforced gate (no pre-commit hook, not enabled in qlty), so the warnings are IDE-only but still noisy.

Suggested Fix: Ship "MD024": { "siblings_only": true } and "MD060": false in the template's .markdownlint.json, so the planning/ADR templates lint clean out of the box.

Affected Files: .markdownlint.json

docs.title: .claude/settings.json Bash permission colon syntax is wrong
docs.text:
• Priority: High
  • Category: Configuration
  • Discovered: 2026-06-01

Issue: The template generates "Bash(cmd:*)" entries in .claude/settings.json permission allow lists. Colon syntax is the MCP tool format; shell commands require space syntax "Bash(cmd *)". All generated entries are silently inert at runtime, meaning every shell command still prompts for permission despite being listed.

Context: Discovered during compliance audit PR #3 review via pr-review skill. All 6 generated entries used colon syntax and were flagged as a Critical finding.

Suggested Fix: Change the template's .claude/settings.json allow entries from "Bash(cmd:*)" to "Bash(cmd *)" (space separator, not colon).

Affected Files: .claude/settings.json

docs.title: mkdocs.yml site_description not quoted, breaks YAML parse
docs.text:
• Priority: High
  • Category: Configuration
  • Discovered: 2026-06-01

Issue: The generated mkdocs.yml has site_description with an unquoted value containing a colon-space sequence (assistant: RAG over...). YAML interprets the unquoted colon-space as a mapping separator, producing a parse error at build time: "mapping values are not allowed here, line 6, column 83".

Context: Discovered when the Documentation CI check failed with a YAML parse error. The mkdocs.yml shipped by the template was the immediate cause.

Suggested Fix: Wrap the default site_description value in double quotes in the template, or use a value that doesn't contain colons.

Affected Files: mkdocs.yml

docs.title: no-em-dash pre-commit hook self-flags its own config file
docs.text:
• Priority: Medium
  • Category: Tooling
  • Discovered: 2026-06-01

Issue: The no-em-dash hook added by the compliance system sets its entry to the em-dash character (U+2014) itself. Because the hook's own definition in .pre-commit-config.yaml contains this character, the hook flags its own config file on every run unless .pre-commit-config.yaml is in the exclude list.

Context: Discovered during pr-fix session for compliance PR #3. Running pre-commit run --all-files would have failed on .pre-commit-config.yaml.

Suggested Fix: Add \.pre-commit-config\.yaml to the no-em-dash hook's exclude pattern. Also check that any CLAUDE.md generated by the template that documents the em-dash rule uses (U+2014) instead of the literal em-dash character.

Affected Files: .pre-commit-config.yaml

docs.title: pr-validation.yml references deprecated org workflow python-pr-validation.yml
docs.text:
• Priority: High
  • Category: CI/CD
  • Discovered: 2026-06-01

Issue: The template generates a pr-validation.yml with a core-validation job that calls python-pr-validation.yml from the org .github repo. That workflow has been deprecated and replaced with a migration stub that intentionally exits 1 with the message "Migrate to python-ci.yml". The SHA pinning by the compliance system locked in the deprecated commit, causing every PR to fail with "Core Validation / This workflow is removed".

Additionally, python-ci.yml (the replacement) uses --no-build during install, which is incompatible with editable installs. The core-validation call provides duplicate value since ci.yml already runs Python tests and quality checks.

Context: Discovered during compliance audit PR #3 review and subsequent pr-fix. Two fix attempts were needed: first migrating to python-ci.yml, then removing the job entirely when the install compatibility issue surfaced.

Suggested Fix: Remove the core-validation reusable workflow call from the template's pr-validation.yml. Keep it focused on PR-specific checks (changelog, dead code, link check). Python quality checks belong in ci.yml.

Affected Files: .github/workflows/pr-validation.yml

docs.title: validate-front-matter hook scans the whole docs/ tree, so latent failures block unrelated commits
docs.text:
• Priority: High

Issue: The validate-front-matter pre-commit hook sets pass_filenames: false and runs python tools/validate_front_matter.py docs, so it re-validates the entire docs/ tree on every trigger instead of only the staged files. The shipped docs/ADRs/adr-template.md (schema_type: adr) and the brainstorming spec under docs/superpowers/specs/ (no front matter) are both non-compliant with the validator out of the box, but the failure stays latent because the hook only triggers on a non-planning docs/*.md change. The first unrelated doc commit surfaces all of them at once and is blocked by files the author never touched.

Context: Committing two new docs (a compliance report and template feedback) failed the hook with errors pointing at adr-template.md and the superpowers spec, neither of which was staged.

Suggested Fix: Either honor staged filenames (pass_filenames: true and validate only the passed paths), or ship the template's own docs/ files already compliant with the validator. A hook should not fail on files outside the current change set.

Affected Files: .pre-commit-config.yaml, tools/validate_front_matter.py, docs/ADRs/adr-template.md, docs/superpowers/specs/*.md

docs.title: Front-matter schema lacks ADR statuses and a report/lessons-learned doc type
docs.text:
• Priority: Medium

Issue: The discriminated-union schema in tools/frontmatter_contract/models.py allows only status values draft, in-review, published, and schema_type values script, knowledge, planning, common. The shipped adr-template.md uses status: proposed (a standard ADR lifecycle state) and schema_type: adr, both rejected by the schema. Separately, CommonFM sets ConfigDict(extra="forbid"), so generated report artifacts (for example compliance lessons-learned files carrying session_date, repo, manifest_version) cannot be made compliant without stripping their metadata into the body.

Context: Found while making adr-template.md, a compliance retrospective, and a brainstorming spec pass the validator. The ADR template ships failing its own schema.

Suggested Fix: Add ADR lifecycle states (proposed, accepted, superseded, deprecated) to the status literal, add an adr (or report) schema_type variant, and either relax extra="forbid" for report types or exclude generated report trees (like docs/planning/ is already excluded).

Affected Files: tools/frontmatter_contract/models.py, docs/ADRs/adr-template.md

docs.title: validate-cruft.yml is a legacy check that blocks PRs on template drift
docs.text:
• Priority: High
  • Category: CI/CD
  • Discovered: 2026-06-08

Issue: The shipped .github/workflows/validate-cruft.yml runs cruft check on every push and pull request and exits non-zero when the project has drifted from the template, failing CI to "prevent merging out-of-date code". This cruft-drift gate is a legacy mechanism that is no longer used: projects intentionally diverge from the template over their lifetime (re-scopes, custom architecture), so a hard PR-blocking drift check produces a permanent red check unrelated to the PR under review.

Context: Discovered on PR #5 (a two-line renovate.json change). The validate-cruft check went red purely because the template advanced since the project was generated, blocking an unrelated config PR. The check is not part of the org ruleset's required status contexts, so it is advisory noise rather than an enforced gate.

Suggested Fix: Remove validate-cruft.yml from the template entirely. Template-sync is already handled by the two-part .standards/ baseline system plus the /merge-standards flow, which does not require a blocking CI check. If a drift reminder is still desired, ship it as a schedule-only workflow that opens a tracking issue (the existing schedule branch already does this) and drop the push/pull_request triggers so it never blocks a PR.

Affected Files: .github/workflows/validate-cruft.yml

docs.title: Submitting Feedback
docs.text:
Once you've collected feedback, you can:

  1. Create an issue in the cookiecutter-python-template repository
  2. Submit a PR if you have fixes for the template
  3. Share this file with the template maintainers

When submitting, reference this project as the source of the feedback.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Records (ADRs)
docs.text: This directory contains Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) that document important architectural and design decisions made in MTG AI.

docs.title: What is an ADR?
docs.text:
An Architecture Decision Record (ADR) is a document that captures:

  1. The Decision: What architectural choice was made?
  2. The Context: Why was this decision needed? What problem were we solving?
  3. The Consequences: What are the trade-offs and implications?
  4. Alternatives: What other options were considered and why were they rejected?

ADRs serve as:

  • Historical Record: Why decisions were made and when
  • Knowledge Sharing: Helps new team members understand the architecture
  • Decision Rationale: Documents the reasoning behind choices
  • Future Reference: Helps when revisiting decisions

docs.title: When to Write an ADR?
docs.text:
Write an ADR when you need to document:

  • Major architectural choices
  • Technology selection decisions
  • API design decisions
  • Infrastructure decisions
  • Dependency choices
  • Testing strategies
  • Any decision that impacts multiple components

docs.title: ADR Format
docs.text: All ADRs follow a consistent structure with YAML front matter. See adr-template.md for the complete template.

docs.title: Front Matter
docs.text:
---
schema_type: adr                          # Always "adr"
title: "ADR-NNN: Short descriptive title"
description: "Brief one-line description"
tags:
  - architecture
  - decision
  - area-of-concern
status: published                         # proposed, published, deprecated, superseded
owner: "core-maintainer"                  # Primary decision owner
authors:
  - name: "Author Name"
purpose: "Why this ADR was written"
---

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
1. Status: Current status (proposed, published, deprecated, superseded)
  2. Context: Why this decision was needed
  3. Decision: What decision was made and how
  4. Consequences: Positive and negative impacts
  5. Alternatives: Options considered and why rejected
  6. Implementation: How the decision was implemented
  7. Performance Benchmarks: Metrics and measurements (if applicable)
  8. References: Links to related documents and code

docs.title: Status Definitions
docs.text:
• proposed: Under consideration, not yet approved
  • published: Accepted and implemented
  • deprecated: No longer used, replaced by newer approach
  • superseded: Replaced by a newer ADR (should reference the new ADR)

docs.title: Naming Convention
docs.text:
ADR files are named with sequential numbers:

  • 0001-adr-title-kebab-case.md
  • 0002-another-adr-title.md
  • 0031-comprehensive-benchmarking-framework.md

Use the next available number and follow kebab-case for the title.

docs.title: Creating a New ADR
docs.text:
1. Use the template: Copy adr-template.md to NNNN-your-adr-title.md
  2. Fill in the sections: Complete all required sections
  3. Request review: Create a PR with the ADR
  4. Iterate: Address feedback and refine
  5. Approve and merge: Once consensus is reached
  6. Update status: Change status from "proposed" to "published"

docs.title: Deprecating an ADR
docs.text:
If an ADR is no longer valid:

  1. Update the status to deprecated
  2. Add a deprecation notice at the top
  3. Reference the replacement ADR if applicable

Example:

> **DEPRECATED (2025-11-17)**: This approach is no longer used.
> **Reason**: Replaced by more efficient algorithm
> **Reference**: See [ADR-032: Better Algorithm](0032-better-algorithm.md)

docs.title: Superseding an ADR
docs.text:
If a newer ADR replaces an existing one:

  1. Update the old ADR status to superseded
  2. Add a supersession notice referencing the new ADR
  3. The new ADR should reference the old ADR in Alternatives section

docs.title: Examples from MTG AI
docs.text:
The repository may contain several example ADRs:

  • Infrastructure Decisions: Platform choices, deployment strategies
  • Architectural Patterns: Design patterns and architectural approaches
  • Technology Selections: Framework, library, or tool choices
  • API Design: API structure and design decisions

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Make Decisions Explicit: Even if a decision seems obvious, document it
  2. Include Trade-offs: Explain what we're gaining and what we're sacrificing
  3. Be Concise: Focus on the essential information
  4. Use Examples: Include code samples or diagrams when helpful
  5. Reference Alternatives: Show that alternatives were considered
  6. Document Implementation: Explain how the decision was actually implemented
  7. Update When Needed: If a decision changes, update the ADR

docs.title: Linking to ADRs
docs.text:
When referencing ADRs in code or docs, use:

See [ADR-001: Decision Title](docs/ADRs/0001-decision-title.md) for details.

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
For questions about a specific ADR, reach out to:

  • ADR Author: Listed in the authors field
  • Owner: Listed in the owner field
  • Team Discussion: Open a GitHub Discussion

Reference: This ADR process is based on Nygard's ADR format with modifications for MTG AI.

docs.title: ADR-NNN: Short Descriptive Title of the Decision
docs.text:
Status: proposed → Change to published once approved, or deprecated/superseded if no longer valid

For help with this template, see ADRs/README.md

Decision Date: YYYY-MM-DD Deciders: List of people who made this decision Affected Teams: Teams impacted by this decision Implementation Target: When this will be implemented (if applicable)

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
Describe the problem or situation that prompted this decision. Include:

  • What is the problem?: Clear description of the issue
  • Why is it important?: Why does this need to be solved now?
  • Who is affected?: Which teams or components are impacted?
  • What are the constraints?: Budget, time, technical limitations?

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
Describe the current situation before this decision:

  • Existing approach: How is it currently done?
  • Current limitations: What are the problems with the current approach?
  • Why it's not sufficient: Why can't we continue with the status quo?

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
List the requirements that informed this decision:

  • Must have: Non-negotiable requirements
  • Should have: Important but not critical
  • Nice to have: Desirable but not required

Example:

  • Must have: Backwards compatible with existing APIs
  • Must have: No external service dependencies
  • Should have: <100ms latency per operation
  • Nice to have: Self-documenting code

docs.title: What We Decided
docs.text:
Clearly state the decision made:

We will [action/choice] using [approach/technology]

Example:

We will standardize on Pydantic v2 for all data validation, using discriminated unions for polymorphic type handling.

docs.title: Why This Decision
docs.text:
Explain the key reasons for this choice:

  1. Reason 1: How it solves the main problem
  2. Reason 2: Additional benefits
  3. Reason 3: How it meets requirements

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Provide implementation details:

  • High-level approach: Overview of the solution
  • Architecture: Component interactions and data flow
  • Integration points: How it connects to other systems
  • Configuration: Key settings and customization options

docs.title: Pseudocode/Example
docs.text:
# Example showing how the decision is implemented
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field

class User(BaseModel):
    """User model with validation."""
    name: str = Field(..., min_length=1)
    email: str = Field(..., regex=r'^[^@]+@[^@]+\.[^@]+$')
    age: int = Field(..., ge=0, le=150)

# Usage
user = User(name="John", email="john@example.com", age=30)

docs.title: Positive Consequences
docs.text:
What benefits does this decision provide?

  1. Benefit 1: Specific improvement and impact
  2. Benefit 2: Technical or organizational advantage
  3. Benefit 3: Long-term value

docs.title: Negative Consequences
docs.text:
What trade-offs or challenges does this introduce?

  1. Challenge 1: Specific limitation or drawback
  2. Challenge 2: Learning curve or migration effort
  3. Challenge 3: Ongoing maintenance requirement

docs.title: Neutral Consequences
docs.text:
What aspects are neither good nor bad?

  1. Change 1: No impact on specific area
  2. Change 2: Depends on future circumstances
  3. Change 3: Requires different approach elsewhere

docs.title: Alternative 1: [Name/Description]
docs.text:
Approach: How this approach would work

Advantages: - Specific benefit 1 - Specific benefit 2

Disadvantages: - Specific drawback 1 - Specific drawback 2

Why Not Chosen: Clear explanation of why this was rejected

docs.title: Alternative 2: [Name/Description]
docs.text:
Approach: How this approach would work

Advantages: - Specific benefit 1 - Specific benefit 2

Disadvantages: - Specific drawback 1 - Specific drawback 2

Why Not Chosen: Clear explanation of why this was rejected

docs.title: Alternative 3: [Name/Description]
docs.text:
Approach: How this approach would work

Advantages: - Specific benefit 1 - Specific benefit 2

Disadvantages: - Specific drawback 1 - Specific drawback 2

Why Not Chosen: Clear explanation of why this was rejected

docs.title: Implementation Steps
docs.text:
1. Phase 1: First step and timeline
  2. Phase 2: Second step and timeline
  3. Phase 3: Final step and timeline

docs.title: Code Example
docs.text:
Show how the decision is implemented in actual code:

# Concrete example of the implementation
class YourComponent:
    """Component implementing the ADR decision."""

    def __init__(self, config: YourConfig):
        """Initialize with configuration."""
        self.config = config

    def process(self, data: InputData) -> OutputData:
        """Process data using the decided approach."""
        validated = self.validate(data)
        result = self.transform(validated)
        return result

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Document any configuration options:

# config.yaml
decision_option_1: value
decision_option_2: value
nested:
  option_3: default_value

docs.title: Migration Path (if applicable)
docs.text:
If this replaces existing functionality:

  • Phase 1: Support both old and new approaches
  • Phase 2: Deprecate old approach with warnings
  • Phase 3: Remove old approach entirely

docs.title: Performance & Metrics
docs.text: If applicable, document performance implications:

docs.title: Performance Benchmarks
docs.text: Metric Before After Impact Operation latency XXms XXms YY% improvement Memory usage XXMb XXMb YY% reduction Throughput XX/sec XX/sec YY% improvement

docs.title: Validation Approach
docs.text:
How will we validate that this decision achieved its goals?

  • Benchmark against baseline metrics
  • A/B testing approach (if applicable)
  • User acceptance testing
  • Load testing results

docs.title: Related ADRs
docs.text:
• ADR-001: Previous Decision (replace with actual ADR link) - Related context
  • ADR-005: Future Decision (replace with actual ADR link) - Builds on this ADR

docs.title: External References
docs.text:
• Technology/Framework Documentation
  • Research Paper or Article
  • Related GitHub Issues

docs.title: Implementation References
docs.text:
• Implementation File: src/component.py (replace with actual path)
  • Test Coverage: tests/test_component.py (replace with actual path)
  • Configuration: config.yaml (replace with actual path)

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Q: When should we reconsider this decision? A: If [specific condition] changes, we should revisit this ADR.

Q: How does this relate to [other system]? A: It integrates via [specific interface/API].

Q: What if the assumption [X] proves false? A: We would [specific fallback plan].

docs.title: Discussion Points
docs.text:
• Key assumptions that should be validated
  • Open questions for future consideration
  • Feedback from stakeholders

docs.title: Appendix A: Research & Analysis
docs.text:
Include any research done to inform this decision:

  • Comparative analysis of options
  • Survey results
  • Performance testing results
  • Industry best practices

docs.title: Appendix B: Examples from Similar Projects
docs.text:
Cite how other projects or organizations have handled similar decisions:

  • Project X approach and results
  • Project Y lessons learned
  • Industry standards and practices

Last Updated: YYYY-MM-DD Reviewed By: List of reviewers Next Review: YYYY-MM-DD (or "On demand" if no scheduled review)

docs.title: Compliance Retrospective 2026-06-01
docs.text: Repo: ByronWilliamsCPA/MTG_AI | Session date: 2026-06-01 | Manifest version: 2026-05-30

docs.title: Session Summary
docs.text:
Metric Value Repos reviewed 1 Total findings (net of overrides) 72 Critical 9 Important 37 Suggested 26 Unclassified candidates 11 Remediated in session 38 Deferred 8 Overrides applied 0

PR opened: https://github.com/ByronWilliamsCPA/MTG_AI/pull/3

docs.title: 1. Template artifact residue (5 of 11 unclassified candidates)
docs.text:
Every finding below traces directly to the cookiecutter scaffolding step, not to developer drift. The template emitted these problems into a freshly generated repo:

  • FOUND-CHANGELOG-001: CHANGELOG references Poetry instead of UV (1 repo; systematic in all UV-based template outputs)
  • FOUND-PKG-001: pyproject.toml missing project.urls and has empty keywords (confirmed in 3+ cookiecutter-python-template scaffolds)
  • FOUND-PKG-002: Incomplete Python version classifiers in pyproject.toml (1 repo; template gap)
  • FOUND-README-002: README footer ends with an incomplete sentence (1 repo; template boilerplate defect)
  • FOUND-README-003: Badge URLs use lowercase repo name; actual repo uses mixed case (1 repo; template variable interpolation gap)

Root cause: The cookiecutter template does not drive project.urls, keywords, Python classifiers, or badge URLs from context variables at generation time. The CHANGELOG boilerplate still names Poetry as the package manager.

Template feedback filed: All five items added to docs/template_feedback.md.

docs.title: 2. qlty silent-skip wrapping (3 checks: PC-003, CI-007b, PC-005)
docs.text:
The scaffolded .pre-commit-config.yaml and associated qlty integration wrapped basedpyright, ruff, and several linting hooks in configurations that caused qlty to silently skip rather than execute them. The pattern is:

# Problematic pattern
- repo: local
  hooks:
    - id: basedpyright
      entry: bash -c 'basedpyright "$@" || true'  # silent pass-through

When a hook is wrapped in || true or otherwise made to always exit 0, the pre-commit gate becomes cosmetic. The scaffold's qlty integration was the source: qlty's shim layer generates silent-skip behavior for hooks it does not natively support rather than failing audibly.

Impact: PC-003 (basedpyright absent/silent), PC-005 (ruff silent), and CI-007b (hook name mismatch) were all remediated in this session. Any repo generated from this template before the fix carries the same exposure.

Template feedback filed. The qlty integration config needs explicit fail_fast: true and a documented fallback policy.

docs.title: 3. CI-022/023: Required status check names do not match workflow producer names (deferred)
docs.text:
The org branch-protection ruleset specifies required status check context strings (Security Analysis / Security Gate Validation, Check REUSE Compliance) that do not match the name: fields of the workflow jobs that are supposed to satisfy them. This means the ruleset does not actually gate merges on those workflows for any freshly scaffolded repo until the names are manually reconciled.

Pattern: Every repo generated from this template and added to the org before the ruleset context strings are corrected will have an effectively unenforced security gate. This is a systemic scaffolding + org-config alignment gap, not a per-repo quirk.

Status: Deferred (CI-022, CI-023). Requires coordinated change to either the workflow name: fields or the org ruleset context strings. The deferred items are tracked in PR #3.

docs.title: 4. REUSE.toml merge not performed at scaffold time (FOUND-REUSE-002)
docs.text:
The template generates a .standards/REUSE.baseline.toml file but does not merge it into REUSE.toml automatically. The merge step is documented as a manual developer action. As a result, newly scaffolded repos have no SPDX headers in src/. REUSE CI fails until the developer runs the merge.

Unclassified candidate: FOUND-REUSE-002 (No SPDX headers in src/).

docs.title: 5. Noxfile stub sessions advertised but not implemented (FOUND-NOX-001, FOUND-NOX-002)
docs.text:
The scaffolded noxfile.py declares perf and mutate sessions that raise NotImplementedError, and does not include bandit or pip-audit sessions. Running nox -l presents these as available, creating false confidence.

Pattern: Confirmed in 3+ repos generated from this template.

docs.title: 6. Deprecated version: key in Compose files (FOUND-DOCKER-002)
docs.text:
The scaffolded docker-compose*.yml files carry the top-level version: key, which Docker Compose v2 ignores with a deprecation warning. All generated repos exhibit this.

Pattern: Confirmed in 3+ repos generated from this template.

docs.title: 7. known-vulnerabilities.md location mismatch (FOUND-OSSF-002 variant)
docs.text: The CLAUDE.md standard references docs/known-vulnerabilities.md but the template places the file at the project root (known-vulnerabilities.md). Any audit script or CI check that looks for the canonical path will report a false absence.

docs.title: Proposed Manifest Additions
docs.text: The following patterns each appear in 3 or more cookiecutter-python-template scaffolds. They are promoted to manifest candidates.

docs.title: Candidate 1: FOUND-PKG-001 -- Missing project.urls and empty keywords
docs.text:
- id: FOUND-PKG-001
  domain: foundations
  severity: suggested
  description: "pyproject.toml is missing [project.urls] table and/or has empty keywords list"
  verify: "toml_key_exists: pyproject.toml [project.urls]"
  remediation: >
    Add a [project.urls] table with at least Homepage and Repository entries.
    Populate the keywords list with 3-5 relevant terms.
  override_eligible: true

docs.title: Candidate 2: FOUND-NOX-001 -- Noxfile advertises unimplemented sessions
docs.text:
- id: FOUND-NOX-001
  domain: toolchain
  severity: suggested
  description: "noxfile.py declares sessions (perf, mutate) that raise NotImplementedError"
  verify: "grep_absent: noxfile.py NotImplementedError"
  remediation: >
    Either implement the session bodies or remove the stub declarations.
    Stub sessions listed by `nox -l` create false confidence that checks run.
  override_eligible: true

docs.title: Candidate 3: FOUND-DOCKER-002 -- Deprecated version: key in Compose files
docs.text:
- id: FOUND-DOCKER-002
  domain: foundations
  severity: suggested
  description: "docker-compose.yml carries a top-level 'version:' key deprecated in Compose v2"
  verify: "grep_absent: docker-compose*.yml ^version:"
  remediation: >
    Remove the top-level 'version:' key from all Compose files.
    Docker Compose v2+ ignores it with a deprecation warning.
  override_eligible: true

docs.title: Candidate 4: FOUND-REUSE-002 -- No SPDX headers in src/
docs.text:
- id: FOUND-REUSE-002
  domain: ossf
  severity: suggested
  description: "Source files in src/ lack SPDX-License-Identifier and SPDX-FileCopyrightText headers"
  verify: "reuse lint: exit 0"
  remediation: >
    Run `reuse addheader --license <SPDX-ID> --copyright '<holder>' src/**/*.py`
    and merge REUSE.toml from .standards/REUSE.baseline.toml.
  override_eligible: false

docs.title: Candidate 5: FOUND-NOX-002 -- Noxfile lacks bandit/pip-audit session
docs.text:
- id: FOUND-NOX-002
  domain: toolchain
  severity: suggested
  description: "noxfile.py does not include a security session running bandit and pip-audit"
  verify: "grep_present: noxfile.py pip.audit"
  remediation: >
    Add a 'security' nox session that runs `uv run bandit -r src` and
    `uv run pip-audit`. Wire it into the CI nox invocation.
  override_eligible: true

docs.title: Agent Scope Expansion Candidates
docs.text:
• foundations-auditor: Consider adding a check for pyproject.toml [project.urls] table presence and non-empty keywords (currently only checks [build-system] and [project] top-level keys).
  • toolchain-auditor: Consider adding a check for noxfile sessions that contain NotImplementedError or pass as their entire body (stub detection).
  • docker-auditor (or foundations-auditor): Consider adding a check for the deprecated version: key at the top level of any docker-compose*.yml file. This is a zero-false-positive grep.
  • ossf-auditor: Consider expanding REUSE coverage to detect when .standards/REUSE.baseline.toml exists but the merge into REUSE.toml has not been performed (i.e., src/ headers are absent while the baseline is present).

docs.title: High-Frequency Existing Checks
docs.text:
The following checks each fired in this single-repo session and are worth prioritized remediation tracking for any additional cookiecutter-generated repos:

Check ID Description Severity Status CI-022 Required status check context name mismatch (security gate) Critical Deferred CI-023 Required status check context name mismatch (REUSE) Critical Deferred CI-005 Mutable @main refs in org workflow callers Critical Remediated CI-031 Egress-policy block missing (domain audit needed) Important Deferred CI-062 merge_queue ruleset change Important Deferred OSSF-001 Manual web action (OpenSSF scorecard enrollment) Important Deferred GEN-002 reuse.yml CI workflow absent Important Deferred GEN-005 REUSE.toml not merged from baseline Important Deferred

Checks CI-022 and CI-023 are particularly high risk: they mean the org branch protection ruleset is not actually enforced on this repo until the context names are aligned. Any additional scaffolded repo in the org likely has the same gap.

docs.title: Fleet-Wide Actions Required
docs.text:
No fleet-wide patterns detected.

This session covered one repo. No existing manifest check reached the 3-repo threshold required for fleet-wide escalation. The three template-pattern candidates (FOUND-PKG-001, FOUND-NOX-001, FOUND-DOCKER-002) are proposed for manifest addition above; once added and confirmed in subsequent audits they may reach the escalation threshold.

docs.title: Deferred Items Tracker
docs.text:
The following findings were explicitly deferred in PR #3 and require follow-up:

Check ID Reason for Deferral Recommended Next Action CI-022 Required status check name mismatch (security gate) Coordinate with org-admin to align ruleset context strings or rename workflow job CI-023 Required status check name mismatch (REUSE) Same as CI-022; can be done in one PR CI-001/CI-002 Org-reusable workflow conversion too large for this session Schedule as standalone task; high value for fleet consistency CI-003/CI-013 sonarcloud.yml absent; missing workflows SonarCloud onboarding required; schedule with team CI-006 harden-runner ordering Low risk; fix in next CI maintenance pass CI-031 Egress-policy block (needs domain audit) Run network egress audit before adding block policy CI-062 merge_queue ruleset change Coordinate with org-admin OSSF-001 OpenSSF scorecard enrollment (manual web action) Assign to repo owner; 15-minute task GEN-002 reuse.yml CI workflow Implement after REUSE.toml merge (GEN-005) is complete GEN-005 REUSE.toml not merged from baseline Run /merge-standards before next CI push CI-054 Codecov data absent (no CI runs yet) Will auto-resolve once CI runs; revisit in 1 week

docs.title: Template Feedback Summary
